It’s meant to be a good time guys.

MY

And I also love that you worked as a server for 10 years because I feel like you had a front row seat to a lot of these attitudes about wine. And I’m also curious, working in entertainment, if there are any learnings from that space that you feel like you’re able to bring over here or kind of vice versa.

EB

Yeah. I mean, I think, well, for sure I would say as a producer, writer, director, we’re constantly as a company in my entertainment company, we have to take the seat of an idea every time out and bring together all the right collaborators, put together the entire package, think about who the consumer is for it, sell the whole idea with marketing and then put it out into the world. So that’s a skill set that as a producer of content, I’ve been doing now for going on, I don’t know, almost 20 years. And so that skillset lent itself pretty one to one directly across when it came to building a wine brand and building a brand generally. You have to build out pitch perfect with your who is this for? Who would I want to invite into this? What is it? Et cetera. So there’s a lot of one to one there.

But at the very base level, it’s the storytelling about human connection and how do we create a shared experience for the quote audience, the consumers. And just bringing that sense of irreverence and comedy, which I think is very sticky to what I do. That’s who I am as an entertainer. And I think it’s that side of myself is what I’m bringing pretty fundamentally to Archeroos because we are trying to be disruptive in a category that did take itself really seriously. And we think really desperately needed a lighter touch to sort of connect with consumers as you’ve been saying where they are. And so I just feel like whether I’m stepping into this fun character of Marian’s thorn on her side, sidekick, co-owner of Archer Roose Wines, I love being that character to her and annoying the hell out of her. I like being that character, but it’s really just about, we’re just trying to create what are some memorable storytelling elements that we can bring to a category that has really never done what we’re doing.

We just want to make luxury wine genuinely fun.

MY

And so looking ahead, what’s exciting to you about what you guys might be getting into in the future? Does it occur to you that there might be a brand new format that we haven’t even conceived of yet that’s going to happen? When you look out at the horizon of Archeroos, what are you excited about?

MLW

Well, there’s a few different things that we’re excited about. First, we’re excited about what’s working. 25% of our growth comes from just increased velocity from accounts that have been with us year over year. So that means that people are trying us and then every year those accounts are growing. And we’ve seen that across the wholesale channel. And then of course through our partnership with JetBlue and now through our new partnership with Breeze Airways. We got one projection for the year and our partnership’s a couple months old and we’ve already exceeded it. So I am going to touch on something that you said just now, which is I know that there is a thought in this industry that there’s a recognition that we have to find an alternative to the 750 milliliter glass bottle, but maybe that alternative format doesn’t exist yet.

I would push back on that and say instead, we have to listen to what the consumer wants and rise to the occasion of that consumer. And the consumer has been unequivocal in its movement to cans across soda, beer, spirits, which people never thought would happen. And the reason why they’re saying that they’re raising this question about format is because when the strategics got into canned wine, it was a failure because they didn’t pay attention to how canning wine is different than bottling wine. And that was a big question that the brewers grappled with in the early 2000s cracked because of consumer demand. And so my challenge to the industry is to say, let’s actually give the consumer what they want and instead ask us how to be able to deliver a dangerously delicious glass of wine to that consumer for whenever they want it. And in the process we can unlock new occasions and I truly believe we’ll welcome a new Renaissance era into the industry.

MY

Love to hear that because sometimes I don’t hear… I mean, often I don’t hear that actually because I talk to a lot of people in the industry and there’s such a sense of doom and gloom sometimes. And seeing such a big success story and leaning into what the consumer has been telling you unequivocally what they want and are interested in is refreshing. So I love to hear that.

MLW

Now we just need the industry to partner with us in order to help accelerate I think this movement. Because that’s fundamentally what it is.

EB

I think movement is such a good word. I know we’ve talked about how this, we don’t see this as a trend. We think that really this is, it’s like a behavioral solution for a generation that wants to drink less, but drink more intentionally. Whether that’s about moderation or occasion or convenience. I think that’s what’s been really exciting for us is seeing and then how many solutions we provide for people who want to have a fun glass of wine. Yeah.

MY

Right. And I think that also being able to cater to and be mindful of those drinkers who do want to drink less, they’re still invited to the party too. Rather than be like, “Why aren’t they drinking more? This is ruining everything.”

MLW

Exactly. And the reality too is that we’re still comparing ourselves to what the industry did in 2020 when we were all drinking our faces off and that was not sustainable. So this is really just saying like, listen, we are all healing still I think from COVID in a lot of ways. And one of the reasons why we have been so dedicated to the on – premise is, well, there’s the business reason, which is that our format allows us to go to accounts where wine has never played before and be a part of consumer stories in a way where like wine didn’t show up. And that is a massive financial opportunity. But then there’s the emotional because ultimately we’re all in wine because we are delusional romantic suckers at the end of the day and it’s about connection. And for us, the on – premise is about community and being able to scale our community as fast as we can. And that’s because I think we’re still healing from being isolated.

EB

It is a giant collective trauma.

MLW

Yes.

EB

Totally. And Marian and I really connected during that collective trauma. And it was drinking the wine. I’ve tell this story, but it was like I had moved my family to Utah in the pandemic because we could be outside a lot. And I was floating down a river, drinking the wine, the bubbly dipped in the river and keeping the can cold. And it was like, this is amazing.

MY

And is that how you got connected? You were like, “I have to be in touch with this. “

EB

Yeah. We got connected through some business associates, one of whom texted me and said, “I have this woman reaching out about canned wine and I feel like don’t take this the wrong way. That’s totally you. ” And I though, “You know me so well, friend in business.” Yes, it is amazing. It’s amazing.

MY

Oh my gosh. Yeah. I think the industry is being reborn a million times over, especially with the pandemic and love chatting with two women about this. And so I think also going back to the Chardonnay thing, it’s like, “My mom drank this Chardonnay.” There’s also been this association in the industry that things that women like are bad.

MLW

Completely.

EB

Don’t get us started.

MLW

Yeah. Don’t get us started on this. But the point is, is that they control more purchasing power than ever. So give the woman what they want.

EB

That’s how you make the money.

MY

Yeah. They’re going to show up and spend. So stop ignoring them. Final question because my coworkers really wanted to know what type of wine do you think they drink in the district? I’m so sorry. 

EB

First of all, in District 12, they’re like, they got nothing. I mean, they’re making – Toilet wine? Yeah, they’re drinking toilet wine. Exactly. They’re making it themselves at home. But in district two, we’re in the Capitol. The Capitol has it all. The Capitol has it all and they’re walking around with their canned wine through the streets for sure. Yeah.

MLW

Absolutely.

EB

Yeah. Love that. For sure. President Snow’s drinking our bubbly on the daily.

MY

Love to hear it. Oh, one more. This is for me. This is a for me thing. What about Avery Jessup? What is her go – to?

EB

Oh, Avery Jessup would be thrilled about this upcoming Chardonnay launch. Let me tell you her wine. It’s like we made it for Republicans. Let’s just say that. We were looking for a wine for our Republican lady friends. This is our wine. Oh my God.

MY

That’s so funny. Yes. I love the storytelling that you bring to the table, it’s amazing.

MY

How do you feel about canned wine? Do you think the wine industry is doing a good enough job meeting consumers where they are? We’d love to know. You can email us your comments and questions at podcast@wineenthusiast.com. Remember, you can subscribe to this podcast on Apple, Spotify, and anywhere else you listen to your favorite shows. You can also go to wineenthusiast.com/podcast for more episodes and transcripts. I’m Maria Yagoda. Thanks for listening.

The post ‘It’s Meant to Be a Good Time, Guys’: Elizabeth Banks Is Over Wine Snobbery appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.

title: This Charming City in Champagne Is a Winemaker-Loved Culinary Hotspot
summary:
Quieter than the more-famous Reims, Épernay has an eclectic food and drink scene that the region’s winemakers love.

The post This Charming City in Champagne Is a Winemaker-Loved Culinary Hotspot appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Champagne’s identity has long been split between two cities: Reims and Épernay. Reims has the grand Gothic squares and urban edge, while Épernay is quieter, shaped by vineyard slopes and the Marne River.

In Épernay, the connection to the land behind some of the world’s most coveted wines feels far more direct. A patchwork of vineyards appears almost as soon as you exit the train station, and some of Champagne’s most celebrated wine villages sit within just a few miles of town—close enough to reach easily by bicycle.

For anyone who has returned to Épernay regularly over the past five years, the city’s transformation is hard to miss. Not so long ago, a walk along the chalk-white Avenue de Champagne—which forms part of Champagne’s UNESCO-listed landscape—could feel surprisingly sleepy. Today, the doors of its prestigious Champagne houses are open wide, with each looking for new ways to impress visitors.

On that same avenue is the recently opened Musée du Vin de Champagne et d’Archéologie Régionale, set inside the eclectic former mansion of the Perrier-Jouët family. The modern, interactive museum offers a concise way to trace 2,000 years of regional history while also unpacking the technical side of Champagne production.

Épernay is gaining new energy, with ambitious restaurants, wine bars, and four- and five-star hotels opening year after year. Champagne, of course, is not a single style but many: chalky and electric from Avize, generous and red-fruited from Bouzy, vinous and earthy from Les Riceys. That range calls for an equally eclectic food scene. As the winemakers who know Épernay best make clear, there is no shortage of places to experience it—from an apéritif before dinner to a long meal after touring the historic chalk cellars.

Where to Eat in Épernay

The town has its share of beloved dining institutions, and La Grillade Gourmande is one of them. Lyon-born chef Christophe Bernard, who worked with the legendary Paul Bocuse, moves from table to table with his servers, presenting signature dishes such as pigeon with foie gras in puff pastry, venison in pepper sauce, and sea bass with fennel. Everything is cooked in the homey dining room, over an open wood-burning fireplace.

“For me, this is Épernay’s standout bistronomic restaurant. I love the wood-fired cooking, which brings tremendous character to the dishes, as well as the chef’s distinctive personality that comes through on every plate,” says Sacha Geoffroy of Champagne Geoffroy, based in Aÿ.

Her father, Jean-Baptiste Geoffroy, once told me about Chez Max, another lively institution on the banks of the Marne that has been welcoming guests for nearly 80 years. At nearby tables, locals from Champagne recall coming here as children and now return with children of their own. Franck Benard and his wife deliver classic French service with real flair, turning tableside steak tartare into a small performance and theatrically presenting a platter of local cheeses. The cooking is generous, built around seasonal ingredients and deeply flavored sauces. If you see the escargot pie on the menu, don’t hesitate.

Before turning to the city’s more cosmopolitan side, two more addresses for classic French cooking come recommended by Odilon de Varine, cellar master at Champagne Gosset, based in Épernay.

“L’Oben is a very recent addition to Épernay and remains something of a hidden address,” he says. “With only a handful of tables, it offers an intimate atmosphere and serves classic French cuisine executed with great finesse.” Think crisp tarte fine with Bouzy Grand Cru snails, followed by farm-raised pork so tender it spent eight hours getting there.

For a more refined dining experience, de Varine recommends Why Not, especially before an evening at the theater. “The restaurant works with regional ingredients but approaches them with the precision and ambition of a gastronomic restaurant,” he says. The room pairs warm wood, high ceilings, muted tones, and low lighting, while the menu leans into French comfort dishes: think aged Comté ravioli gratin and half-roasted Camembert with Marc de Champagne to start, followed by knife-cut Charolais beef tartare.

A break from the classics comes at the hip, locals-loved Sacré Bistro, where the menu includes a signature burger with melted Comté, alongside other seasonal comfort plates. “The wine list there is one of the most interesting in the region,” says Geoffroy. “You’ll find everything from outstanding Champagne gems to rarer wines from around the world that can be difficult to find elsewhere.”

She also recommends stopping by Cook’in, where chef Guillaume Rousseau brings together French aesthetics and Thai flavors, with dishes such as duck foie gras paired with green apple and ginger confit, and grilled beef tournedos with red curry sauce. “It’s a rather unique address in Épernay,” Geoffroy says. “The Thai cuisine is consistently well-executed, and the contrast between these vibrant Asian flavors and the carefully curated Champagne selection works remarkably well.”

The Asian thread continues at L’Eurasienne, a family-run restaurant where bò lúc lắc is served alongside excellent pizzas. It is a particular favorite of Étienne Calsac, a grower-producer based in Avize. “What I appreciate most is that the wine selection is thoughtful and effective, offering good choices without being overly complicated,” he says. Think bún with lemongrass-grilled pork, or a pizza topped with white asparagus, and 24-month-aged Parmigiano Reggiano.

Where to Drink in Épernay

On Avenue de Champagne, one address stands apart from the polished tasting lounges of the Champagne houses. At Bar Brut, a small family-run bar, you won’t find the Grandes Marques. Instead, the focus is on grower friends such as Vincent Charlot, Louise Brison, and Franck Pascal, whose stories the team is happy to share while filling your glass to the brim. Take a seat in the carefully tended inner garden and, after paying Paris prices, prepare to get emotional: a glass of 2017 vintage blanc de blancs costs €7.50.

For an evening drink, Calsac recommends Le Pif Bar. The atmosphere is pleasingly countercultural, with barrels in place of tables and darts played over a glass of Champagne. “It’s a relatively new wine bar that opens only at night and has quickly become part of the local wine scene,” he says.

When Geoffroy meets friends for a glass, she often chooses 75cl, right in the heart of Épernay. “I really like this place for its dual identity as both a wine shop and a wine bar,” she says. “The selection is always thoughtfully curated, with excellent Champagne producers alongside strong choices from other regions. The atmosphere is simple, warm, and welcoming.”

  • Planning a trip to Napa? Make sure to avoid these common travel mistakes.
  • Here is everywhere to eat, drink, and stay in Seoul.
  • Next time you’re in Italy, skip Venice, and head to Friuli-Venezia Giulia for a slew of culinary gems.
  • WE Executive Editor Maria Yagoda went to a wellness resort—for the wine.

title: Luminous Bubbles with Mediterranean Spirit: Introducing Esprit de Cote des Roses
summary:
Luminous Bubbles with Mediterranean Spirit: Introducing Esprit de Cote des Roses Sparkling or Rosé, Why Choose? This Dazzling French Bottle Does It All Popping the cork on a bottle of sparkling rosé is always a good idea. Whether sharing a meal with friends or winding down after work, this refreshing style of wine makes even

The post Luminous Bubbles with Mediterranean Spirit: Introducing Esprit de Cote des Roses appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Luminous Bubbles with Mediterranean Spirit: Introducing Esprit de Cote des Roses

Sparkling or Rosé, Why Choose? This Dazzling French Bottle Does It All

Popping the cork on a bottle of sparkling rosé is always a good idea. Whether sharing a meal with friends or winding down after work, this refreshing style of wine makes even the smallest moments feel special. One of France’s most iconic producers just released a brand-new expression. Esprit de Cote des Roses, the latest creation from the team at Gérard Bertrand is crafted in the sun-drenched south of France. This captivating sparkling meshes Mediterranean spirit with timeless savoir-faire, all wrapped in an eye-catching bottle that’s certain to leave an impression.  

For over 10 years, Gérard Bertrand’s Cote des Roses still rosé has captured the hearts of industry professionals and wine lovers alike, thanks to its juicy fruit-forwardness and palate-cleansing acidity. After establishing its status as a benchmark reference for French rosé, the visionary brand sought to create its first-ever sparkling edition of the wine, with the estate’s next generation, Emma Bertrand, at the helm of the project.

Designed to elevate the everyday, this enchanting bottle of sparkling rosé delivers both on quality and taste, with its striking, rose-imprinted bottle as the finishing touch— conceptualized by none other than Emma Bertrand herself. Esprit de Cotes des Roses is born from a blend of Chardonnay and Pinot Noir, two of France’s most classic grapes for sparkling wine production, along with the addition of local Grenache, which adds a unique Mediterranean flair to the traditional assemblage. 

Just the heart of the press is used in the juice, promising purity and finesse in each estate-produced bottle. A small amount of reserve wine is blended into the mix, bringing depth and complexity with brioche-driven undertones. After resting in barrel for several months, the wine undergoes a secondary fermentation in bottle before aging sur-lie and bottling with a small amount of dosage. A pale pink hue, the wine reveals subtle aromas of freshly cut red berries and white flower petals, leading to a lively and joyful palate marked by a delicate, creamy mousse and a long and refreshing finish—one distinctly attributed to the cool, high-altitude Pyrenean hills where the fruit is carefully grown.

Moreover, Esprit de Cote des Roses’ structured-meets-easygoing profile makes it perfect for enjoying across an array of jovial moments. The wine’s innate fruit-forwardness and high levels of acidity make it ideal for pairing with an array of dishes, from crisp, seasonal salads and cheese boards to zesty ceviches, salty potato chips, fried appetizers, and spicy curries alike.

Despite its immense food-friendliness, Esprit de Cote des Roses can equally stand on its own, making it just as reliable of a choice for drinking solo as it does for pairing with food. 

Above all, Esprit de Cote des Roses captures the essence of the sun-drenched south of France, where cool mountain air meets the salty Mediterranean Sea. It’s a wine designed to inspire convivial memories, seamlessly turning the ordinary to the exceptional with the simple pop of a cork. 

To learn more about Esprit de Cote des Roses, visit https://www.gerard-bertrand.com/en or follow @thecotedesroses on Instagram.

title: The Best Wines for Every Regional Style of American Barbecue
summary:
From Carolina whole hog to Texas brisket, every type of barbecue dish deserves a great wine pairing.

The post The Best Wines for Every Regional Style of American Barbecue appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Beer may be the typical beverage of choice for America’s big barbecue holidays, but in-the-know drinkers are well aware that an aptly paired wine can take grilled or smoked meat to the next level.

“There are so many great ways to work wine into really any type of cuisine, but particularly with barbecue because there are so many cultural similarities between the way that wine gets made and enjoyed and the way that barbecue gets made and enjoyed,” says Matthew Brown, a wine educator and consultant in Virginia.

Both wine and barbecue take time and skill to make, boast subtle flavor nuances, and highlight regional variation.

Some meats are spice-rubbed. Some are smoked. Some come covered with a sauce, while others boast crunchy, flavorful bark. But regardless of the cut of meat or how it’s made, “you’ll want a wine that can carry you through the whole meal,” says Dana Beninati, a food and wine educator in New York City and the host of Dine With Dana.

Barbecue and Wine Pairing 101

There are some key characteristics worth looking for in a bottle regardless of what specific type of American barbecue you’re enjoying. Start with wines that are refreshing, skew on the lighter side, and are well-balanced.

“I’m looking for [wines with] great acidity and low to medium alcohol but also intensity of flavor that is thirst-quenching,” Brown says. “Because to me, as a Southern boy, I think the quintessential barbecue drink is sweet tea. So, I want my wine to have that same light, refreshing, easy-to-drink quality—but also bold flavors.”

Beninati agrees that fresh, light, and flavorful are the common themes to seek out, and a significant indicator of those qualities in wine is high acidity. “Richness and fattiness play a huge part in barbecue, and we all know how we feel after we eat a really rich, heavy meal,” Beninati says. “You need something refreshing to create balance for the richness of the barbecue.”

Abe Zarate, wine director at Crane Club in New York City, suggests choosing something chilled, like a lighter-style red wine, white, or rosé. “Chillable reds are a big thing right now,” Zarate says. “But I would argue any red could be chillable if that enhances the experience. It’s more about digging deeper into what you’re consuming.”

Sparkling wine is also a great pairing partner for most famous regional barbecues on menus. “The undertones that every barbecue pit master plays around with—smoke, spice, caramelization, fire, browning—work so well with sparkling wine,” Beninati says. “So, it’s a very safe option.”

Below, Beninati, Brown, and Zarate share their favorite pairing partners for some of the most popular styles of U.S. barbecue.

Eastern Carolina Whole Hog with Vinegar Sauce: Cava

Down in the eastern side of Tar Heel State, whole hogs are smoked over a pit. When the meat is done, it’s chopped up, with the white and dark parts mixed together, then soaked in a tangy vinegar sauce. The thin and sharp sauce—which highlights the smoky and savory notes of the pork—is similar to the vinegar-based Escabeche that’s commonly found on top of grilled meats and fish in Spain, says Benanti.

This is why she suggests pairing it with a Spanish Cava. “It’s so elegant but still an easy sipper that can balance out the flavors of barbecue,” she says. “And the price point isn’t aggressive. I am blown away by the quality when we’re talking value for money.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Juvé y Camps 2021 Reserva de la Familia Xarel·lo-Parellada-Macabeo (Cava)

Lifted aromatics carry into the palate on a silken mousse and vibrant acidity. Citrus blossom, passionfruit, and crushed almonds gain freshness with a sprinkle of sea salt and a long finish. 91 Points — Elaine Chukan Brown

$23 Wine.com

Carolina Gold Pulled Pork: Vouvray

When looking for barbecue in South Carolina, the glorious mustardy Carolina Gold sauce is usually stacked high on plates. Pulled pork is covered in a mustard-based sauce mixed with vinegar, brown sugar, pepper, and spice that offers a tangy and spicy flavor and hints of sweetness.

Search for a slightly off-dry Chenin Blanc, like those made in France’s Loire Valley, to play up the sweetness of the sauce. “Wouldn’t it be fun to have something with the right amount of acid but also a little touch sweet?,” says Brown. “Because you’re going to get a lot of acid with this wine, which I think you need when there’s that much intensity of flavor in the sauce.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Domaine Bourillon d’Orleans 2023 La Coulée d’Argent Chenin Blanc (Vouvray)

This delicate wine opens with enticing aromas of white tea, dried field grass and honeyed golden apple accented by orange blossom. The layered palate looks out into a late-summer meadow and frolics on a blanket of orchard fruit that offers savory hints that are complemented by a dollop of orange marmalade on toast. Deliciously attractive. 93 Points — Reggie Solomon

$22 Elk Wine

Alabama Chicken with White Sauce: Petit Manseng

It’s all about the white sauce in Alabama, particularly in the bustling city of Birmingham. A creamy and tangy condiment, primarily made with mayonnaise, vinegar, and pepper, the sauce provides a rich flavor that accentuates the smokiness of the chicken. It’s a perfect match for a textural Petit Manseng. “You need something with texture and body that’s not necessarily full but has that backbone of structural acid that can reset the palate and prepare you for another bite of that rich, smoky chicken,” Zarate says.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Clos Regain 2023 La Côte des Brumes Petit Manseng (Jurançon Sec)

A rich, full wine from a single vineyard, this has a fine balance between dryness and touches of honey. Made from Petit Manseng, the wine is rich and dense, delicious to drink now. Editors’ Choice. 93 Points — Roger Voss

Inquire with the importer

Memphis Dry Ribs: Carbonic Red Blend

One of the most known and popular styles of American barbecue is Memphis dry ribs coated in a blend of spices including paprika, garlic powder, onion powder, black pepper, and sometimes a bit of sugar. It’s an intense bomb of flavor for a sturdy cut of meat that deserves a wine that’s equally flavorful but also refreshing. A carbonic macerated red blend does the trick, according to Brown. “When I think of Memphis barbecue, there’s a little tang,” she says. “And so I like to match that intensity of flavor with a tart freshness and a smoky element.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Itä 2024 Carbonic Zinfandel (Walla Walla Valley (WA))

This unicorn wine undergoes one week of whole-cluster carbonic maceration and two days of post-foot-stomping skin contact. Fermentation and malolactic fermentation are completed in neutral French oak. The Zinfandel’s aromas of lavender and a blueberry muffin with a touch of lemon icing are hunger-pang-inducing. A laser beam of acidity guides lights up the wine’s strawberry and hibiscus flower tea flavors. What a tangy treat! 92 Points — Michael Alberty

$22 Ita Winery

Kansas City Burnt Ends: Sparkling Rosé

There’s a lovely combination of smoky, sweet, and savory flavors in every little piece of Kansas City burnt ends, a staple of Missouri barbecue. It’s made from the marbled tip of smoked brisket, which is coated in a sweet liquid blend of vinegar- and tomato-based sauces. “Let’s pair this with a lower-alcohol option that delivers on major flavors,” Beninati says, noting that the high acidity and effervescence of Pinot Noir-based sparkling rosé acts like a palate cleanser to prep for the next bite.

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Ultraviolet NV Sparkling Rosé Pinot Noir (California)

Tiny fine bubbles swirl throughout bright aromas of raspberry, Bing cherry, cotton candy, and florals on the nose, with a mid-palate filled with citrus zest, tart red apple, and skins on the finish. Best Buy. 91 Points — Tonya Pitts

$22 Ultraviolet Wines

St. Louis Ribs with Tomato Sauce: Franciacorta

It’s all about the sweet, tomato-y sauces slathered on baby back ribs in St. Louis, Missouri. These ribs are known for their tender meatiness and are smothered in a sweet, tangy, and slightly smoky sauce that enhances the natural taste of the pork. Beninati reaches for a glass of Franciacorta, Italy’s answer to Champagne. “I’m out in the sunshine; I’ve got my sunglasses on; I’ve got sauce on my face,” she says. “I’m reaching for a glass of this sparkling because it has so much structure to stand up to the rich boldness of this meat.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Fratelli Berlucchi NV Brut 25 Chardonnay (Franciacorta)

This steely blanc de blanc lifts out of the glass with crisp orchard fruit, fresh jasmine and lemon zest aromas giving way to subtle hints of brioche. The palate shows green apple, mixed citrus and hints of pineapple, finishing with a mineral edge. 91 Points — Jeff Porter

$52 Wine-searcher

Central Texas Brisket: Italian Cabernet Sauvignon

When it comes to barbecue, brisket is king. And nowhere does it quite like Central Texas. With a simple seasoning blend of black pepper, salt, and a pinch of cayenne, the complex flavors come from the low and slow cooking process—usually about 12 hours indirectly smoked with post oak. The final result is a meat with a juicy center enveloped in a dark, crunchy, spiced bark.

It needs a sturdy wine that can “complement every bite in any cut of the brisket: lean, fatty, or both,” like a Cabernet Sauvignon-heavy red blend from Bolgheri, Zarate says. “Grapes grown in this area are influenced by the coastal climate to the west and a natural mountainous amphitheater surrounding it,” he adds. “The breezier microclimate translates to a bold wine that offers ripe dark fruit with a backbone of fresh acidity.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Grattamacco 2023 Rosso Red Blend (Bolgheri)

The nose is earthy and umami, with aromas of damp soil, herbs, coffee, pencil shavings and blackberries. Sweet and tart blackberries are layered with herbs on the palate, while the finish is hot and peppery. Tannins are magnificently unapologetic, and there’s a sense of anticipation around what the wine will become when they begin to relax. 96 Points — Danielle Callegari

$32 Station Plaza

Santa Maria Tri-Tip: Cabernet Franc

On the West Coast, barbecue lovers revel in Santa Maria tri-tip, a hunk of beef that’s prepared with a basic blend of salt, pepper, and garlic salt. It’s grilled on a grate that gets lowered and raised above red-oak embers, which gives a crisp crust, juicy center, and smoky flavor. Brown recommends pairing it with a wine with a little “smoky, spicy kind of element,” like a Cabernet Franc. “It’s not overripe, it’s not big, it’s not jammy,” says Brown. “It’s really well-structured. It has beautiful tannins, and you could serve it at about 60 degrees or so. I think it shows well with just a little bit of chill on it.”

Wine Enthusiast recommends:

Luna Hart 2023 Honeybear Orchard Vineyard Cabernet Franc (Los Olivos District)

The dense and dark core of berry and plum is cut by a fiercely spicy streak of cracked pepper on the exciting nose of this bottling. Snappy flashes of plum and elderberry pop on the sip, where that pepper adds both brightness and complexity to the tense, lingering mouthfeel. 95 Points — Matt Kettmann

title: AI Makes Information Cheap. Taste Is Priceless.
summary:
AI is extraordinary at synthesizing data and information. But the best recommendations in life come from real-life experience and hard-won expertise. I recently came back from a trip with my husband that we planned entirely on our own. Like many people, I leaned heavily on today’s AI tools: Gemini, ChatGPT, Claude. They helped us organize

The post AI Makes Information Cheap. Taste Is Priceless. appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
AI is extraordinary at synthesizing data and information. But the best recommendations in life come from real-life experience and hard-won expertise.

I recently came back from a trip with my husband that we planned entirely on our own. Like many people, I leaned heavily on today’s AI tools: Gemini, ChatGPT, Claude. They helped us organize and format itineraries, optimize routes based on proximity and opening hours, and fit everything into the constraints of a typical day.

So, did AI help? Absolutely.

But did AI plan the trip for us? Not even close.

What made the trip unforgettable was our access to more than a dozen trusted experts on food, wine, and travel. I pulled recommendations from no fewer than 15 friends and colleagues whose opinions I deeply respect. AI simply planned an efficient schedule.

That’s because LLMs are only as good as the information and prompts we give them. They’re extraordinary at aggregating knowledge. They are far less capable of using judgment, and that’s an important distinction.

Today, AI is largely fed by the same platforms many of us had already begun moving away from when we require discernment: Google Reviews, Yelp, Reddit, TripAdvisor, and countless other repositories of user-generated content. These systems require colossal amounts of data, and nobody has more data than the collective of millions of people’s opinions.

Which is exactly the problem.

AI is extraordinary at summing up consensus. But the best recommendations in life don’t come from consensus. They come from credibility combined with lived experience.

I’ve always found these databases return the safest recommendations. The internet is optimized for popularity. AI optimized for distillation. Neither optimizes for taste. Creativity, intrigue, and genuine discovery live somewhere else. They live with strong points of view, deep knowledge of hidden stories, and people who know something because they’ve actually lived it.

Anthony Bourdain understood this. Nobody would have watched a television show called Parts Everyone Knows. We crave the unknown. The hole-in-the-wall restaurant. The producer who makes only a few thousand bottles. The neighborhood bar without a sign. The brain-chemistry-altering dish that changed your friend’s life.

Why, then, would we rely on tools whose very nature is to average those experiences together? To remove all conviction and authenticity? 

This growing distrust of AI’s recommendations isn’t just philosophical. It’s already showing up in the data. Travel agents were supposed to disappear. AI, online booking platforms, and recommendation engines were going to make them obsolete. Instead, the profession is growing. Even our own Cork + Compass Travel has doubled its trips this year, built on the prestige of our real life human wine critics as the guides and hosts. 

According to The Wall Street Journal, the travel agency sector is up 17% since 2023 forecasted to hit $134B in 2026. In addition, LinkedIn listed travel advisors among the fastest-growing jobs. The number of travel advisors jumped from about 190,000 in 2024 to 310,000 in 2025—a remarkable 63% increase in a single year. In a piece from Business Insider, you can see how travel agents are using AI to help them mainly with administrative tasks, rather than the human piece of strong relationships, advocacy, and bespoke experiences.

Why?

Because when every piece of information is available to everyone, information stops being valuable. Judgment becomes valuable. The question isn’t, “What are the 10 most popular restaurants in Madrid?” It’s, “Who’s opinion do I trust to ask?” That’s where wisdom still wins. The same is true in wine. 

Anyone can ask AI to explain Barolo, compare vintages, or summarize tasting notes. But when our Italian critic, Jeff Porter—also the former wine director at Del Posto in NYC—recommends a bottle, I listen. When he tells me about his favorite restaurants and bars in Verona, I go. Jeff’s value isn’t simply that he’s evaluated and tried thousands of wines over decades. It’s that he’s developed a sense of taste that I know I can trust.

AI has democratized knowledge, but it has not democratized taste.

That’s true of all respected wine critics, wine writers, collectors, travel advisors, content creators, chefs, and countless other specialists. Their expertise isn’t becoming less relevant because of AI. It’s becoming more valuable than ever. 

We’re seeing this sentiment for somms too, including this article from The New York Times’ salaciously titled “A.I. is Coming for Sommeliers.” Rather than replacing somms, AI bots are helping guests have deeper, more confident conversations with real-life experts. In the piece, Claudia Rosellini, wine director at Bavel in Los Angeles, says,”It becomes a more specific conversation…instead of me playing 21 questions.”

In fact, judgment may be becoming even scarcer. The AI era is making all of us more capable generalists. It can make us a jack of all trades in almost any subject. But mastery—a skill built from decades of obsession, experience, and conviction—remains stubbornly human and hard won. That’s why wine’s emphasis on expertise, storytelling, and authenticity will not be replaced by any intelligence that’s artificial at its core. 

Jacqueline Strum

President & Publisher of Wine Enthusiast Media

title: Maceration 101: What Every Wine Lover Should Know
summary:
Maceration is like steeping tea, but instead of a teabag, it’s grape solids soaking into juice, imparting color, tannins, polyphenols, and flavor.

The post Maceration 101: What Every Wine Lover Should Know appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Key Points

  • Maceration—skin contact before and during fermentation—extracts color, tannins, aromas, and structure, defining a wine’s style and texture.
  • The length of maceration shapes wine character: shorter contact creates lighter wines, while extended contact builds deeper color, firmer tannins, and complexity.

If you’ve ever wondered why your glass of red wine looks more like a glass of purple ink, the answer is maceration. Likewise, if you have ever tasted a red wine and felt as though there were sandpaper stuck to the roof of your mouth, the reason is also… maceration.

Put simply, maceration is when the juice from the crushed grapes stays in contact with the skin before and during fermentation. Think of it like steeping tea, but instead of a teabag, it’s grape solids soaking into juice, imparting color, tannins, polyphenols, and flavor along the way.

How Maceration Impacts Wine

While not often used in white wine production, this step is essential for red wines. Without maceration, you’d basically have something closer to white wine, as for most red wine the juice itself is usually clear. Think about a Blanc de Noirs Champagne. Those are Champagnes that are white in color but made from red grapes that do not go through a maceration process.

Rosé wines just get a quick soak, with perhaps a few hours to days of maceration. The style of rosé will determine if it is just a quick hit (lighter wines that have a hint of color) or if everyone stays in the pool together a bit longer (darker wines with just a bit of tannin). The extent of maceration here is displayed in the difference between a delightfully pink Côtes de Provence and a darker, almost brooding Tavel rosé. 

Orange wines follow the rosé path, as they are produced with white grapes that see extended skin contact. They’re deeper in color than your average Pinot Grigio and have more texture, often boasting a slightly savory edge. Most white wines skip extended maceration altogether to enhance the freshness and acidity, and to avoid any grip and or color added by the skins.

Short vs. Extended Maceration

Timing is pivotal when it comes to this intricate process. Short maceration leads to lighter, softer profiles in wines such as Pinot Noir, Blaufrankisch, or Gamay. Leave those skins in contact with the juice longer and you start building deeper color, firmer tannins, and potentially more structure, as you’d expect from something like Cabernet Sauvignon or Nebbiolo.

But it is not just about how long maceration lasts—the actual skins matter, too. Thin-skinned varieties like Pinot Noir don’t give up as much color or tannin, no matter how long you leave them to party with the juice. Those thick-skinned grapes like Tempranillo or Syrah can turn that clear juice into an opaque barrel of ink in the same amount of time.

Other Types of Maceration

Winemakers also have a few tricks of the trade that they use to impart various characteristics on a specific wine. A cold soak (a pre-maceration, if you will) gently pulls out color and aroma before fermentation turns up the volume. Carbonic maceration (used often in Beaujolais Nouveau) ferments whole grapes in a sealed carbon dioxide-rich environment, creating bright, juicy wines that will often elicit notes of candy and bubble gum. Meanwhile, extended maceration keeps the juice and skins hanging out in the pool way after hours. Even after fermentation is finished, this can soften potentially harsh tannins while adding depth and intense color.

The big takeaway here? Maceration is a balancing act and an intentional tactic based on the grapes being fermented and the winemaker’s vision. The more time the juice and skins spend bonding, the more color, tannin, and intensity we see in the wine. Less maceration time, on the other hand, keeps things fresh and vibrant. 

So the next time you take a sip and your glass and teeth are stained a deep purple, know that it all comes from how long the winemaker let those grape components hang out together before moving the juice along to the aging stage of the winemaking process.

title: 7 Up-and-Coming Wine Regions That Should Be on Your Radar
summary:
With shifts in climate, consumer behavior, and politics continuing to seesaw the playing field for winemakers worldwide, these are seven regions to watch.

The post 7 Up-and-Coming Wine Regions That Should Be on Your Radar appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Despite a laundry list of recent challenges facing those who make and sell wine, the wine industry is always, in some way, expanding. 

Long-established regions are proving nimble, making bold strokes to align themselves with current consumer behavior while still maintaining a foothold in tradition. Meanwhile, other regions and countries that would have been considered up-and-coming just a handful of years ago are moving beyond their opening gambits, demonstrating both depth and breadth in their winemaking. 

With shifts in climate, consumer behavior, and politics continuing to seesaw the playing field for winemakers worldwide, these are seven wine regions to watch right now. Not all of these areas have wines that are available broadly, but they are worthy of your attention, especially if your travels bring you within striking distance (or at least put you in front of a local wine list).

Picpoul de Pinet

That Picpoul de Pinet doesn’t already occupy more shelf and emotional space in the American market is surprising. A subappellation of Southern France’s Languedoc, whose rosé wines we have already begun to favor as a budget-friendly alternative to Provence, it’s the kind of zesty, value-driven white wine that has been rising in popularity, coupled with a distinctive, signature bottle design. Previously enveloped by the catchall appellation Coteaux de Languedoc, Picpoul de Pinet AOC was officially codified in 2013, adopting the consumer-friendly spelling of “Picpoul” for the appellation, rather than that of its grape, Piquepoul Blanc. 

Mercifully, it is one of few regions in France to lead with the actual name of the grape, adding to its consumer appeal. “It’s so understandable,” says Alexandre Freguin, award-winning French sommelier and Picpoul de Pinet ambassador. “It’s one style of wine, one color, one grape variety.” Plus, considering the regional marketing slogan “son terroir c’est la mer,” (its terroir is the sea), the wine has “the taste of holidays,” he says, “where you can easily picture the vines growing right by the Mediterranean sea.” 

More than just being a great, under-the-radar white, as a still-young appellation within France, Picpoul de Pinet shows how it is possible for French regions to honor tradition while meeting the current cultural moment, anticipating the future, and driving the appellation to even higher levels of quality. 

The AOC recently mandated lighter versions of its signature “Neptune” bottles, decreasing the bottle weight by over 20% while preserving the appellation’s striking embossed seal. At the same time, Picpoul’s producers have begun experimenting with a new qualitative label indication, called “Patience,” which now accounts for 1% of the region’s production. Patience indicates Picpoul de Pinet wines that have extended lees aging, adding to their complexity and gastronomic capacity and marking them as potentially age-worthy. 

“Picpoul de Pinet is really taking into consideration not only what’s happening now,” says Freguin, “but what will happen next.”

Bolivia

With Argentina, Chile, and even Uruguay moving beyond novelty and affordability in the eyes of global wine consumers, Bolivian wine has entered the picture at an entirely different level than its South American predecessors.

As the sole importer of Bolivian wines to the U.S., Llama Wines co-owner Ed Lara says, “There’s no need for Bolivian winemakers to export,” as a majority of the country’s wines are consumed locally, with wine lists in the Bolivian capital of La Paz liberally peppered with native bottles. The few that are available to U.S. consumers tend to be higher end: think light-handed Tannats and unique blends such as Campos de Solana’s Tri Blanco Riserva, which marries Viognier, Riesling, and Moscatel de Moscatel. 

Bolivian wine is buzzy right now in part thanks to some serious star power. Filmmaker Steven Soderbergh’s recent foray into Bolivian grape spirit singani helped bring attention to the county’s wines. “He opened the door after bringing it to the Oscars,” says Lara, “so we are obviously kind of riding the coattails of that.”

While we wait for more Bolivian wine to become available, Bolivian wine tourism is on the rise. The recently established Ruta del Vino Bolivia is still relatively under-the-radar, meaning that visitors can expect authentic, uncrowded experiences. On top of that, Bolivia has some of the world’s most unique vineyards, from the “arbolita” or forest vineyards of the Cinti Valley, with vines that climb the local pink peppercorn trees, to the mountainous vines of Tarija, which are among the highest elevation vineyards in all of the Americas.

Essex

Outside of England, English wine may be synonymous with English sparkling wine, as its Champagne-style sparklers are what initially put the country on the world wine map. But England is much more than a one-trick pony, and its still wines are now demanding their due, especially in Essex, where the microclimate defies Great Britain’s reputation for dampness. 

East of London and north of the famed sparkling counties of Kent and Sussex, “all things being relative in the United Kingdom, Essex is exceptionally dry,” says John Atkinson, MW, and viticulturist for Danbury Ridge Wine Estate. Due to its estuarine valleys that don’t even develop much cloud cover, “I don’t even think there’s anywhere drier in France,” he says, “and the light levels are really quite high.”

The fair weather in Essex is combined with a mineral-rich clay bedrock called smectite that both collects water in preparation for drier vineyard days ahead, and then swells at the surface to prevent roots from becoming waterlogged. The conditions are proving especially ideal for the region’s Chardonnay. In a blind tasting of Chardonnay organized at 2026’s London Wine Fair, featuring peak bottles from around the world, Danbury Ridge’s 2023 Octagon Block Chardonnay came in third. (Of note: Australian Chardonnays placed both first and second.)

This also means that Kent and Sussex are to Champagne what Essex potentially is to Burgundy; just as Champagne’s winemakers have begun producing wine in England, as with Taittinger’s Domaine Evremond, Essex is also becoming an alternative place to plant for Burgundy producers, without the constraints of land availability and appellation law. 

Essex is far from the only place in England producing still wine worth consideration, but it is the natural bellwether, and hopefully what moves English wine beyond its reputation for being a land that’s solely good for sparkling. “It’s so important going forward for England to actually have still wine to show its competence, that it’s a proper wine region,” says Atkinson. “Partly because every wine region produces sparkling, I don’t know if sparkling wine actually cuts through in the same way that still wine does.” 

Exclusive Access to
Wine Country,
Led by Wine Enthusiast

China

There’s no question that Chinese wine is seriously on the rise—but don’t make the mistake of thinking it’s just one thing. Among the country’s dozen distinct wine regions, “there’s a huge difference in terms of altitude, a huge difference in terms of latitudes and daylight hours, and a huge difference in terms of soils as well,” says Michael Palij, MW, and founder of importing business Winetraders. From Ningxia, where vines are buried in the winter to stave off the extreme cold, to more temperate coastal and southern regions, such as Shandong and Yunnan, there is enormous nuance among China’s wine regions. “If you look at the lunacy of the ultimate expression of terroir—Burgundy—where you’ve got 600 different appellations or lieux-dits in an area which is only 100 miles long, it’s just so, so important to think of China with these regional differences,” says Palij.

While the top grape plantings are fairly consistent across the country, based on what was initially brought to China for the Sino-French Cooperation Demonstration Vineyard in 2000, there are regional affinities with specific varieties, such as Chardonnay in the cooler, high elevation sites of Yunnan, and Petit Manseng in Shandong’s coastal climate.

“There’s this real thirst for knowledge,” says Palij of the country’s young and eager winemakers, “and I’ve never been anywhere where people who wanted to learn about wine have just taken it so seriously. One of the sommeliers I was working with just rings up Angelo Gaia and goes, ‘I want to come and work with you.’ They want to produce great wines, they’re very fast learners, and they rapidly prototype… If something doesn’t work, they’ll just rip it up and try again.”

Only a small fraction of Chinese wine is currently exported, but the industry is still on an upward trajectory. Chinese wine is making its way to North American shelves and beyond thanks to the efforts of projects such New York’s China Wine Club and sommeliers and wine directors in upscale Chinese restaurants worldwide.

Aosta Valley

Italy’s Aosta Valley may be the country’s smallest wine region both by size and by volume of wine produced, but it nonetheless boasts some notable stats, including the highest number of autochthonous grapes and the highest altitude vineyards in all of Europe. It also remains wide open for discovery, even for its own countrymen. “Sometimes when I travel around Italy and tell people I work in a winery they say, ‘No, it’s not possible, they don’t do wine in Aosta Valley,’” says Martina Fazio, manager of tasting experiences at Grosjean Vins. (On the Alpine border with France, the region is officially bilingual, hence its Francophone name.) “We may be the most unknown wine region in Italy, because everybody comes here for skiing or hiking.”

Those same mountain slopes that beckon travelers for recreational purposes also deliver some dynamic wines. The sheer steepness in Aosta necessitates what is considered “heroic” winemaking, an official term for slopes whose angles and altitudes are challenging on a multitude of levels, particularly come harvest time. Those that persevere, however, offer unique sips from native grapes such as Prié Blanc, Cornalin, Neyret, Petite Rouge, and Premetta. At Cave Mont Blanc, whose vineyards are the highest among the highest, Prié Blanc rootstock were left untouched by phylloxera.

The region is also somewhat of a canary in a coal mine for the potential to grow grapes at even higher altitudes in Europe. Certain areas of Aosta Valley are only permitted to use its various DOP classifications for grapes grown at up to 900 meters of elevation. But they know they can go higher, and want a chance to prove it. “In the past, planting higher was considered something crazy, because you had too much acidity in the wines,” says Fazio. “The DOP was born in a period before climate change. Now, we are facing higher temperatures, so we would love to plant a little bit higher.” 

Producers are working with the slow-moving bureaucracy in order to bring the limit of the DOP up to 1,000 meters. Grosjean and many other producers in Aosta are waiting to plant higher until the starting gun is fired. Meanwhile, Rosset Terroir produces a non-DOP white wine from Petite Arvine called Sopraquota 900 (meaning “above the 900-meter limit.”)

North Carolina

In the post-firework haze of America’s 250th birthday, let us consider one of this country’s oldest wine-producing states. While North Carolina may not be the oldest wine-producing region in the land that currently encompasses the United States, (that distinction belongs to New Mexico), it is one of the originals in the very notion of U.S.-made wine. The state boasts what is arguably the original American winegrape variety, Muscadine, which is still growing from the Mother Vine on North Carolina’s Roanoke Island.

Despite North Carolina’s viticultural and geological longevity, anchored by ancient Appalachian bedrock, it is finally coming into its own. “We are not trying to be California, Oregon, Washington, or Virginia,” says JW Ray, owner and winemaker for JOLO Winery & Vineyards, located in the state’s largest AVA, Yadkin Valley. “The region has moved from potential to proof. Ten or fifteen years ago, many of us were still pioneers, learning which varieties belonged here, how our weather patterns behaved, and how our soils and elevations translated into wine. Today, the best producers have more tenure with their vineyards. We understand the land better, the vines are more mature, and the wines are becoming much more site-specific.” 

This effort has nonetheless expanded North Carolina’s offerings, rather than narrowing them. Wines are made in every style—alongside other important fermented beverages for the state, such as mead and cider—and from grapes across numerous species: vitis vinifera, French-American hybrids, native vitis rotundifolia, and labrusca-type. 

Wine tourism in the Yadkin Valley has surged. Outside of Asheville, Biltmore Estate, a historical mansion and winery built in the late 1800s for George Washington Vanderbilt II, “welcomes more than one million visitors each year, providing an incredible opportunity to introduce guests from across the country to North Carolina wines,” says Sharon Fenchak, winemaker for Biltmore Winery.

“The biggest change I see is expectation,” says Ray. “Years ago, many guests arrived surprised that North Carolina wine could be serious. Now they arrive curious and increasingly informed. They may still be discovering us, but they are no longer shocked that the wines are good. That is a major shift.”

Morocco

Winemaking in Morocco predates Islamic rule, and began during the time of the Phoenicians, with a boom during the European phylloxera epidemic during the mid-19th century and later as a French protectorate. A confluence of factors created a unique position for Morocco among winemaking countries: a complicated relationship with the ruling culture, where drinking alcohol is all but prohibited for its citizens, combined with prevailing European influence. But Moroccan wine is more than just a cultural curiosity, and poised to both evolve and grab more attention in the years to come, propelled by growing tourism.

After a period of winemaking freefall following France’s exit in the 1950s, wine in Morocco was revived in the 1990s with an influx of foreign investment and a push by King Hassan II. Its trajectory since then has placed it neck and neck with Algeria as the next-highest wine producing country in Africa behind South Africa, featuring predominantly Mediterranean red grapes. With its unique terroir including both Atlantic and Mediterranean coastal influence, plus the impact of the Atlas mountains, some winemakers are starting to shift its identity toward something in and of itself, rather than that of a European relic. Native grapes are slowly but surely resurfacing, such as crisp and floral Faranah. Vin gris, a pale rosé made from minimal skin contact, is a local signature.

The European AOC and AOG classifications left behind may also be falling out of favor. “Personally, I don’t think those classifications bring anything to the wines in the way they are articulated in Morocco, without much context from wine specialists,” says Nahla Bahnini, CEO and owner of Domaine de Baccari.

A significant rise in tourism to the country in recent years—a more than 50 percent increase between 2019 and 2025, according to the Ministry of Tourism, Handicrafts, and Social and Solidarity Economy—has introduced visitors from around the world to Moroccan wine and its capacity for quality. “Visitors want to have a local experience,” says Bahnini. “When they want to have the local couscous, or one of the tagines, they want to accompany it with Moroccan wine.”

title: What the Supreme Court’s Ruling on Roundup Means for Wine (and the World)
summary:
A legal and political battle surrounding Roundup, a glyphosate-based herbicide used across viticulture and found widely in wines, has just wrapped. Here’s why you should be paying attention

The post What the Supreme Court’s Ruling on Roundup Means for Wine (and the World) appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
Key Points

  • The Supreme Court ruled 7–2 for Bayer/Monsanto, blocking additional Roundup cancer warning lawsuits.
  • Over 100,000 lawsuits prompted $10+ billion in Bayer settlements despite potential links between glyphosate and cancer.
  • Glyphosate was found in 19 of 20 wines and beers tested, with 81% of Americans showing recent exposure.

A highly controversial debate surrounding regulation of a popular herbicide wrapped its time in the Supreme Court late June.

The case, Monsanto v. Durnell, centered around whether Monsanto, an agrochemical corporation owned by German pharmaceutical and biotechnology company Bayer, is liable for failing to warn consumers of cancer risks. 

In a 7-2 opinion, Supreme Court Justices sided with Monsanto, agreeing to shield the agricultural giant from future liability against those claiming its weed killer, RoundUp, caused adverse health effects.

After an intense, decades-long debate, a wide range of disparate players are angered by the ruling, from environmental justice and consumer advocacy groups, like Climate Justice Alliance and Earthjustice, to the “Make America Healthy Again” movement (MAHA).

The Trump administration stood with Bayer in the case. “A lot of MAHA voters are realizing they’ve been snookered, they’ve been had by Republicans that had no intention of protecting their health,” says David Murphy, spokesperson for MAHA, founder of United We Eat, and finance director of Kennedy’s presidential campaign, to The Hill. “It’s just a talking point that they added.”

Here’s how it all went down—and what it means for wine drinkers.

The Case Basics

Monsanto v. Durnell stems from a 2019 Missouri case filed by gardener John Durnell, who alleges he developed non-Hodgkin lymphoma after decades of exposure to RoundUp, a Monsanto-owned herbicide. He and his legal team argued that the company is liable for damages for failing to warn consumers of the potential health risks of its main ingredient, glyphosate.

Monsanto has been involved in well upwards of 100,000 cancer lawsuits in the last ten years, with more than 60,000 cases pending. Despite the difficulties in directly connecting glyphosate exposure to illness, Bayer has paid out some $10 billion-plus to date to cancer victims.

The ruling in favor of the chemical giant quashed hundreds of thousands of class action lawsuits of individuals who claim to have faced harm after contact with glyphosate-based herbicides. It will save the company billions.

Also on the docket: if cancer warnings should appear on products in general, and whether states can independently mandate health warning labels or if that issue should be addressed federally. 

In opening arguments, justices were divided. Some, such as Justice Brett Kavanaugh, sided with Monsanto’s argument, arguing that labels and bans are outlandish. Others, including Justice Elena Kagan, leaned towards the ruling that the U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (E.P.A.)’s determination of glyphosate safety—and the federal governement—shouldn’t impact state’s rights to legislate.

Ultimately, the majority sided with Monsanto, save for Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson and Justice Neil Gorsuch, who filed dissenting opinions. The majority reasoned that because the E.P.A. concludes that glyphosate is not likely to cause cancer (despite what the International Agency for Research on Cancer says), cancer warnings should not be required. 

They pointed to the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (F.I.F.R.A) preemption clause, titled “uniformity,” that prohibits states from requiring additional labeling on top of the E.P.A.’s. This effectively squashed the option that cancer warnings could be rolled out on a case-by-case basis. 

Those who have campaigned to have glyphosates phased out of agriculture, as well as those who have had their health, or the health of their loved ones, impacted by glyphosates are incensed.

“Eliminating consequences for companies that choose to not properly warn users of the well-known, significant health hazards their products pose does a level of harm to society that is hard to overstate,” says Adam Casto, winemaker at Ehlers Estate in Napa Valley.

Glyphosate in Wine

While the government formally claims consumers need not be warned about these potentially dangerous chemicals, the wine industry has been working hard to make glyphosates less commonplace. 

Many large-scale winegrowers lean on glyphosate to eradicate weeds that compete with vines for water and nutrients in the vineyards. Yet, a growing slew of industry groups have been ringing the alarm on the dangers of glyphosate and are actively working toward phasing it out of the industry.

Some viticultural non-profits, like Napa Green, and regions, including Penedès Denominació D’Origen, are in varying phases of banning the use of these kinds of synthetic herbicides. These efforts are seen as fortifying a commitment to organics, sustainability, and terroir. Glyphosate spraying has been linked to increased earthworm mortality, harming aquatic organisms and bee colonies, and affecting ground and surface waters.

“Consumers need to be aware of the devastating effects on their health and the health of soil,” says Ivo Jeramaz, from Napa’s Grgich Hills Estate. “There is nothing worse for the vitality of your soil than spraying glyphosate. It kills all life, resulting in a fragile, fertilizer-dependent system that is not ideal for the growth of any plant life, including the desired crop.”

And individual industry pros have been ringing the alarm on its impacts on worker health.

Nick Dugmore, who founded Stoke Wines on Australia’s Kangaroo Island, is one of the loudest. In 2022, he fell asleep in a tent in a field beside his vineyard that had been sprayed with the chemical. He awoke covered in rashes all over his body that burned with excruciating pain for 10 days. 

The next year, he was diagnosed with Stage 4 bowel cancer. His doctors estimated the tumor started growing around the time of glyphosate exposure.

He’s not the only farmer or person who even lives near a glyphosate-sprayed farm with that kind of story. Many have reported lesions and rashes after exposure to the chemical that may have resulted in cancer diagnosis. “I’ve had hundreds of messages from people saying they’ve had similar rashes, similar cancer diagnoses,” says Dugmore. 

But, the ease of use and cost has made it difficult for farmers who fall under these rules in some cases. 

In a 2019 landmark initiative, Conegliano Valdobbiadene Prosecco DOCG prohibited the use of glyphosate across the region in an effort to lessen its environmental impact. Producers were forced to adapt. However, it’s proven more difficult than the consortium may have realized. 

“Today, in light of the increasingly unsustainable costs of managing heroic vineyards, some winegrowers are beginning to call for the reintroduction of glyphosate,” says Diego Tomasi, director of the Consorzio di Tutela del Vino Conegliano Valdobbiadene Prosecco DOCG.

Is RoundUp Actually Harmful?

Health debates aside, the topic weaves through volleys of political, social, economical, and environmental debate. 

The MAHA movement—which held a People Vs. Poison Rally outside the Supreme Court—argues that “companies poisoning us should not get immunity when their products cause cancer.” 

In 2025, Senator Cory Booker introduced the Pesticide Injury Accountability Act, to help victims of manufacturer-caused illnesses receive reparations.

“This is definitely one of those ideologies that muster up religious fervour,” says Stephen Scarnato, co-founder of Long Island Vine Care and New Roots Wine.

As with ever-present wine-and-health debates, the science behind the true impacts of glyphosates is conflicting.

The E.P.A. finds “there is no risk to human health when glyphosate is applied following label instructions,” says Exposure Consulting Toxicologist Alex LeBeau. “Simply stated: the dose determines the health risk.”

Environmental, food-safety, and farmworker advocacy groups have long been questioning these claims. Some recent reports seem to be backing them up. 

In December, a prominent 25-year-old paper that’s been cited by researchers as evidence of glyphosate’s safety was retracted by the journal that published it. Newly released emails disclosed Monsanto scientists’ significant role in conceptualizing and writing the study. 

Meanwhile, a growing body of research has linked glyphosate exposure to various cancers including non-Hodgkin lymphoma, and the World Health Organization has classified glyphosate as “probably carcinogenic to humans.”

Glyphosate Is Everywhere

The issue is: Monsanto (now Bayer) is a giant, with strong political and economic sway. People have died arguing over their chemicals. Nefarious allegations against the company overfloweth—everything from bullying scientists to suppressing evidence. Monsanto has a firm grasp on agriculture and glyphosate is, currently, inescapable. 

In 2019, the United States Public Interest Research Group tested through a range of grocery store wines and beers. Of the 20 beverages tested, 19 contained glyphosates. 

Likewise, Dugmore has purchased wines from across the Southern Hemisphere and had them lab tested for glyphosates. Almost all—even ones that have been regenerative for over 15 years—came back testing positive for glyphosates. “The water from that McLaren Vale vineyard is fueled by rainfall, grey water, and creeks,” says Dugmore. “Glyphosates are in those.”

But if that were true—that the chemical runs freely throughout the environment—wouldn’t we see it in our food supply? 

Yes, we do. Over a two year period, the United States Food and Drug Administration found glyphosate in 59% of corn and soy samples tested. The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention estimates 81% of the U.S. population has had recent exposure to it. A study by the U.S. Geological Survey National Water Quality Program detected it in 66 of the 70 streams and rivers it tested. 

It’s also been found in the urine of most farmers—and 81% of non-agricultural workers.

Hard to Quit—Despite the Risks

With so many questions swirling around about its safety, why is glyphosate still so prevalent in the agricultural and wine industries?

Scarnato will argue there are negatives to eliminating glyphosates. After all, they can be inexpensive to use, and can often save farmers time and other costs. Without them, “You’re driving a tractor more, which means more carbon emissions and more labor,” says Scarnato. “And that’s in short supply. It’s going to hurt the bottom line of every winery.”

Farmers and proponents of the herbicide claim that banning it could collapse the world’s food supply.

Several studies, including one from the National Institute of Health, showed that glyphosate bans could devastate agricultural economies and increase production and overall food costs. 

Similarly, a French viticultural report found that replacing herbicides in vineyards with non-chemical would drastically increase farming costs—an added layer of burden to an industry that is already struggling with decreased demand and rising costs. 

In February, President Trump issued an executive order calling for increased domestic production of glyphosate. The order cited pressure on the food supply and national security (one of the main ingredients in the chemical is used in an array of U.S. military munitions). 

Likely responding to backlash from his MAHA base, United States Secretary of Health and Human Services Robert Francis Kennedy Jr. (who previously won a $289 million lawsuit against Monsanto) echoed his support for the order on X. In past years, Kennedy has stated he personally believes glyphosates cause cancer.

He posted, “Unfortunately, our agricultural system depends heavily on these chemicals. The U.S. represents 4% of the world’s population, yet we use roughly 25% of its pesticides. If these inputs disappeared overnight, crop yields would fall, food prices would surge, and America would experience a massive loss of farms even beyond what we are witnessing today. The consequences would be disastrous.”

The E.P.A. is currently facing an October 1, 2026 deadline to re-examine glyphosate’s safety. Now that the Supreme Court case is in the rearview mirror, both the farmers who use the chemical and staunch opponents await the next milestone in the saga.

“Those who have been using it consistently will continue to do so, while those wineries, like us, who are staunchly opposed, will not,” says Jeramaz. “We can only continue to advocate against its use in our communities, and hope that we can effectively support fellow vintners to at least reduce their reliance on it.” 

In the meantime, many industry pros, like Casto, expect third-party certifications like “USDA Organic” to mean more in the future: They “represent an explicit safety value.”

title: Hate Buttery Chardonnay? Try These and Get Back to Us
summary:
With less acid courtesy of malolactic fermentation, these Chardonnays are smooth as butter—with just the right amount of bite.

The post Hate Buttery Chardonnay? Try These and Get Back to Us appeared first on Wine Enthusiast.
content:
While a crisp white is one of the wine world’s perennial favorites, we’re here to make the case for the decadent, rich universe of buttery Chardonnay. 

Few topics are as divisive in the wine landscape. Buttery Chardonnay’s infamously creamy quality is the product of malolactic fermentation, a technique involving introducing specific bacteria to wine. This addition converts tart malic acid into softer lactic acid, lowering the wine’s acidity and resulting in a rounder mouthfeel and a distinctively buttery flavor (thanks to Diacetyl, a byproduct of the process).

While we can agree that buttery Chard may be an acquired taste, some folks absolutely hate it. In our April 2023 print issue, Marshall Tilden III, the President of Wine Education at Wine Enthusiast, came out swinging against buttery Chard, albeit judiciously. “[Unlike] other, and more simple white varietals, the complexities of Chardonnay stand strong on their own without needing to soften, or transform, the flavor profile,” he said. Although malolactic conversion “can round out the acidity, softening the wine on the palate, I much prefer a Chard with searing acidity that has me craving a second sip.”

Team Buttery Chardonnay

On the other side of the spectrum, however, the style isn’t without its fans. 

“In a world that craves unadulterated wines more than ever, denying Chardonnay its natural inclination to undergo malolactic fermentation is an enological faux pas,” argues Matt Kettmann, Wine Enthusiast Writer-at-Large and reviewer of wines from California. “Why rely on modern technology to change the way this noble grape has been treated since Burgundian monks got a hold of it a millennium ago?”

WE Writer-at-Large Tom Capo, who reviews Sonoma County wines, is also a fan. “Sometimes, there’s no substitute for the decadence of a barrel-aged Chardonnay,” he says. “There’s a certain voluptuousness to well-made buttery Chardonnay that just hits different—especially during crab season. The velvety mouthfeel and rounded flavors of this style of Chardonnay accentuate the texture of succulent crab meat.”

Michael Alberty, a WE Writer-at-Large who reviews wines from the Pacific Northwest, similarly adores buttery Chards—with a caveat. “I look for Chardonnays that provide a bit of butter without making me feel like I’ve licked the bottom of a Land O’Lakes tub,” he says. “I’m in heaven if I find a Chardonnay that combines butter with elevated acidity.”

It’s All About Balance

This is all to say: Even those with a preference for buttery styles have their limits. What is most important, as with all wines, is a balanced flavor profile, and malolactic fermentation can play a crucial part in that balance.

“Listen, I love a racy Chardonnay more than most,” Kettmann says. “But the grape’s potential for screamingly high acidity—especially in cool-climate regions like California’s Central Coast—typically requires the smoother mouthfeel and warmer flavors that come from a bit of lactic acid. Like everything in wine, it’s all about finding that balance, and the absolute denial of one critical and natural tool in the winemaking process is the start of a dangerous journey. If you don’t like ML, you may just not like Chardonnay.”

If you’ve gotten this far in the story, we’re assuming you’re Team Buttery Chardonnay (or at least open-minded). To reward you, we’ve compiled a list of highly-rated bottles hailing from California, Australia, and beyond. These bottlings hit the sweet spot, splitting the difference between butter bomb and nose-searing acidity.

Rowen Wine Company 2023 Cooley Ranch Chardonnay (Sonoma County)

Punchy citrus, nectarine, lemongrass, river rock, and white flower aromas explode on the nose of this high elevation Chardonnay. The palate dances with lively acidity and energetic phenolic grip, fading into flavors of focused Meyer lemon, grilled Anjou pear, toasty hazelnut, a whisper of cinnamon, orange blossoms, and chopped lemon verbena along a mouthwatering finish. Stunning now, this will improve with patience. Editors’ Choice. 96 Points — Tom R. Capo

Inquire with the winery

La Crema 2023 Chardonnay (Los Carneros)

Aromas of citrus blossoms, white tea join flavors of spiced apple sauce with a dollop of caramel pudding through a palate washed clean by fresh acidity. Youthfully taut upon opening, this Chardonnay continues to reveal additional flavors and aromas with further sipping. 90 Points — Elaine Chukan Brown

$40 La Crema

Kosmic Kitty 2024 Chardonnay (California)

This wine is a fun and interesting selection because it uses cats. Imagine kicking back on the couch with your furry friend, enjoying a bright, aromatic Chardonnay that tastes like fresh, crisp apple, nutmeg, orange zest, with a touch of lightly toasted oak, and a mineral finish. Best Buy. 90 Points — Tonya Pitts

Inquire with the winery

Felton Road 2022 Block 2 Chardonnay (Central Otago)

Felton Road may be world renowned for their Pinots, but do not ignore their wonderful Chards. After all, if Pinot thrives in Central’s semi-arid, mountainous landscape, so too should Chardonnay. With a few years of bottle age, this is shining. There’s both vibrancy and depth of flavor: roasted cashew, lemon oil, truffle, beeswax, wet stone and a herbal edge. It’s mid weight, curvy; a toasty, flinty fringe around pithy, salted lemon fruit concentration to a long finish. Editors’ Choice. 96 Points — Christina Pickard

$50 Rye Brook Wine & Spirit Shop

Kumeu River 2024 Kumeu Village Chardonnay (Kumeu)

There’s an inviting lift of lemony goodness with a lick of nougat, cashew backbone, hight-toned, with whipped peaks of citrus and cream. A glycerine glide to the palate never feels heavy. In fact, freshness abounds, thanks to a laser beam of acidity that shoots right through the center of the tongue, keeping things tight and focused. Kumeu’s entry level Chard is a wonderful intro to the style, and this particular vintage is a total steal. Stock up, if you can. 94 Points — C.P.

$24 Flatiron Wines

Leeuwin Estate 2022 Art Series Chardonnay (Margaret River)

Straight from first sniff this reels you in. It’s opulent and saline with toasted hazelnuts, creme brûlée, hints of banana leaf, stone fruit, lemon, pineapple rind and a whisper of smoke. It’s beautifully balanced on the palate; quietly powerful; the toasted pastry flavors woven with tangy acidity and an alluring texture. It’s a complete wine now, but should also age gracefully for more than a decade. 96 Points — C.P.

$115 K&L Wines

Snake and Herring 2024 Corduroy Chardonnay (Margaret River)

There’s a lovely bright lemony lift to the nose of this Margaret River Chard, with melon, brine and toasty, cashew notes tucked behind. A line of salt-flecked acidity rushes straight down the middle of the tongue. Modern and approachable, yet also unmistakably “Margs” in style. 94 Points — C.P.

$40 Total Wine

  • Then, take a deep dive into Chardonnayspeak.
  • If you’re ready to do some more shopping, these are our favorite Chardonnays from across the globe.
  • Want to stay stateside? Here are our best California Chardonnays.
  • Or, if you’re feeling the French vibe, these white Burgundies are exceptional.
  • And these Chablis are pure Cha-bliss.
